channel.title: Comments for page "Where Your Eyes Don't Go"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Where Your Eyes Don't Go"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Resurrection has seen better stories.

Now, "Where Your Eyes Don't Go" isn't a bad story, per se. Vang is an interesting character, and his weaponization of cognitohazards is a great fit for this site. The imagery is on-point; in fact, I can clearly visualize just about every scene. The flashbacks also provide excellent characterization and a pinch of lighthearted fun that made me keep reading.

Despite that, however, the story as a whole felt like a droning, dragging series of loosely connected events. There's some weird and ocassionally inconsistent choices that undermine the near-professional quality I expect from this canon - such as using an interrobang twice instead of ?! - and many of the events require an unusually heavy suspension of disbelief given that this is an SCP tale.

For example, there's Ralph and Conwell escaping a locked room, running down a hundred thousand billion trillion stories without being winded, dropping into a manhole before KPA soldiers can stop them, climbing out of the sewer, immediately going to the first unlocked van they see which happens to have the keys lying around, then finding the Foundation pilot unconscious in the backseat who by the way is never mentioned after that. It's far, far too convenient with hopelessly incompetent antagonists (slightly less competent than Ralph) and Vang leaving clues by the dozen. It also reads like Ralph and Conwell's wacky misadventures rather than a relevant Resurrection tale.

Additionally, some of the dialogue also sounds awkward and/or contrived.

+ Example dialogue
– hide block

Ralph's flashlight found a piece of paper taped to the mirror. "Hey, Jacob! I found a suspicious piece of paper!"

"I found a suspicious piece of paper."

Shaking him awake, Ralph tried to speak with urgency. "Hey, I don't know what happened, but we just wasted a day on our backs."

Though he "tried to speak with urgency" ("spoke urgently" sounds more natural), Ralph's dialogue just comes off as mildly confused.

"This could be something… could be nothing, but it could be something."

"Maybe it's a meeting place, and a meeting time."

Could be something, could be nothing, could be something, could be a meeting place, could be a meeting time.

Pulling the vengeance weapon from its hiding place, Ralph opened his mouth to say something cool.

Vengeance weapon? Say something cool? The weak attempt at comic relief here really doesn't belong in a Resurrection tale.

"We're coming to take you away, haha."

"haha".

The dialogue results in the story's pace being both hurried (leaving things unexplored, like Ralph's sister) and slow (providing unnecessary filler and describing every miniscule action). Your tale "I Was Not Magnificent" was written in the same way (indeed, someone else said it left them "more bewildered than entertained"), but it was still more interesting than this one because it involved SCPs rather than Kim Jong Un.

"Where Your Eyes Don't Go" just feels like it was written in one very long, sleepy sitting. It's edited, but given the length it's no surprise that there's still so many clear typos. The idea wasn't bad, but I have to very strongly question the execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
a manhole cover came to his notice.

Quickly lifting the lid

the soldiers balking at jumping after them.

While the tale is charming at points, there are is quite a lot of belief to suspend. This part broke it for me. One-Does-Not-Simply-Lift-A-Manhole-Cover. They weigh over a hundred kilos, and are often friction fit into place. And also, DPRK soldiers, who would throw their own parents into a work camp at the great leader's command, who are probably under the influence of 1427, won't follow fugitives into a sewer?

Also, apparently the foundation falls apart without the key players who are all in Alpha-9. Rather than objecting based on bad possible outcomes, a better objection might have been that it would suck up all the competent personnel leaving other critical areas handicapped for talent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not certain how well this fits in the Resurrection canon. Yes, it has a couple of familiar faces, yes, its central point is solving a mystery from that canon, but otherwise it feels out of place. The other stories are uniformly quickly paced and/or interwoven with the others; in comparison, this feels slow-moving and disconnected from most of that series. If I were looking for a semi-comic one-shot about an agent my age, it's fairly good, just not sure it does much to advance its canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this your show not tell really defines the characters' quirks, feelings, and sensations. The dialogue moves along the plot somewhat, as it should, and also has a purpose whether it's to build the character or their world.

In any other words, I like this.

Take this but not to survive.+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ekblad is a recurring character I use as the commander of Psi-7

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"So, why do you have a Buffalo pin in your hat?

Ralph turned around. There was a young woman there, wearing a Pittsburgh Penguins jersey and peering at his headgear. Her face was a mixture of pity and disgust.

"I don't know if I can associate with a Buffalo fan."

I tend not to comment on anything, but this? So relatable. Bless his lil Sabres fan heart.

Although, to be fair, reading about Agent Ekblad did hurt the immersion for me. Who knows, though? Might be a common last name (or else the skills that turn one into a first overall draft pick in one universe might lend themselves well to the Foundation in another!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In years prior, she might have been scooped up to work with Alpha-9, but several years prior she'd been involved in a breach and rumored to be sympathetic towards the anomaly.

Awkward.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this one could have used a bit more proofreading. Story is good, but some of the errors pulled me out of the immersion a bit. That and the forced "number one with a bullet" reference. That's something I'd expect from Ralph, not the security guy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
WEAPONIZED MEEEEMES!!!

In all seriousness, I find it oddly hilarious that Vang is ultimately defeated because he tries and fails to access the Library. Clearly, he had just enough clearance to know about the Ways without knowing about the whole dumping-Foundation-agents-into-hell part.

item.title: Re: Ralph and Jacob Hit the Road
item.content:encoded: Hey man, I don't need your sass. Those flashbacks are great regardless of my inability to describe why I like them in a non-redundant fashion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
HOW DID I NOT KNOW THERE WAS A TALE WITH DR. BONJOUR

Excellent job, as always. A+. Fun and engrossing, with some truly excellent bits.

"Man." he said, hunching over his desk to finish the work on time. "The Lonely Island speaks to me."

Me too, my dude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Minor typographical stuff I noticed is below! Some of it could be attributed to stylistic choices or dialogue transcription but I wanted to double check.

+ Show 
- Hide 

In Brig, Sub-Basement Sector IV:

Bonjour paused. "Maybe. The devil's in the details with cognitohazard. We have to pick through to make her right again."

Cognitohazard to cognitohazards unless Bonjour is meant to have an accent that loses this distinction. She seems to be pretty concise and terse so it seemed out of character to me.

In TeleConference Room, Research Wing:

Ralph nodded. "Got any wild hypothesis or interesting guesses?"

Hypothesis to hypotheses to match plurality of any.

In Somewhere over Canada:

Sitting in the pilot's seat, a man who knew he was named EKblad

EKblad into Ekblad.

In Kumsusan Palace Of The Sun:

"As I have demonstrated before, the presence of the Stele doesn't mean I defer to your organization's every whim" Kim began snapping his fingers.

I would put a period at the end of this quotation; even though grammatically I'm not sure it's necessary, it adds some hard punctuation to a forceful statement and gives the reveal a little more dramatic weight.

In Ryugyong Parking Garage:

"Sounds like a plan stan."

"Sounds like a plan, Stan" or "Sounds like a plan Stan"; I think that this comes from the name Stan, it looked sort of weird uncapitalized.

Ralph pulled back a piece of opaque glass seperating the cabin from the interior, and saw to his susprise a Foundation Agent lying in the backseat

seperating to separating, susprise to surprise.

More productive thoughts: I enjoyed being briefly drawn back into the Manna Charitable foundation. The characters were evocative on first exposure and the backstories were enlightening. All of the deliberate quirks to the writing (alliteration, assonance, internal rhyme, etc) were entertaining to read, but sometimes if I got too caught up in looking at the structure of the words I started to gloss over what was actually happening.

Strong +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a good example of why I have come to cherish the Foundation. This is as absorbing a study of human nature as anything I've read recently, in a genre and format that I find entirely accessible.

SCP is the New Yorker of weird fiction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's also a reference to End Of Olympians in there if you look real hard at who the dead Prometheus researcher is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
there's a containment here… it's got a Krang in it!

My fucking heart.

I need more Dr. Blast. Also, I really like "She pitied him over her clipboard."

The house of inconsistent horses has a-

Thank god they weren't inconsistent doublehorses, amirite?

Well, that was completely nuts. +1

item.title: Re: Ralph and Jacob Hit the Road
item.content:encoded: Conwell, a fan of recurring flashbacks that provide context to a character? Quelle surprise!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreeing with Scantron when he points out that the ending feels oddly truncated. That being said, this is real fun. Vang's "WEAPONISED MEEEEEEMES" schtick doesn't particularly outstay its welcome, and both Conwell and Ralph hitting his cognitohazard trap is a nice little bit of comic relief.

Neutral vote until the ending gets fuller development.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The content there was cut for stretching on a bit, the story was ready to be closed out here. Thanks for the notice though, I've made edits to reflect the new continuity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this finished? I think there was supposed to be text between the warehouse scene and the scene in the director's office, right? It's weird to have half the events Gillespie described in the final scene to happen offscreen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the madcap tone this piece sets. It's more slapstick than the rest of Resurrection but still manages to maintain tension with an exciting world tour and a terrifically unhinged villain.

item.title: Ralph and Jacob Hit the Road
item.content:encoded:
Holy word count, batman!

Despite its length, I never felt that the story dragged, and all the flashbacks provided for great characterization of why Ralph became the person he is. Additionally, Dr. Vang was a pretty compelling villain, especially with his seeming mastery of setting up congnitohazard traps.

Finally, there place where this tale shines, in my opinion is the banter between all these characters. From Conwell and Ralph being the two dorks who could, to Dr. Vang shouting about his "weaponized memes" there were a lot of gems in the dialogue.

+1. Definitely worth the read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, this took me a long time to write! Thanks to everyone who looked this over during its long gestation and double thanks to Scantron for helping me with Vang and figuring out the endgame of the story. Hope you all find it enjoyable!

This is the last part of a trilogy of stories started with Boss Of Me and continued with its sequel I Was Not Magnificent
